@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/social communication / pragmatic language / social cognition / self-regulation/social filtering / perspective-taking / verbal impulse control / context reading/9-materials-that-help-with-inappropriate-comments#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does my daughter is nine years old, and she says things that make me want to disappear into the floor. it happens constantly, in every social situation, and i never know when the next comment will land. last week at her grandmother's birthday party — a nice family gathering, everyone happy — she announced loudly, 'grandma, why do you have so many wrinkles? you look really old.' the room went silent. my mother tried to laugh it off, but i could see she was hurt. at the grocery store, my daughter pointed at a man in the checkout line and said, clear as day, 'mom, why is that person so fat?' i wanted the ground to swallow me. i apologized, hurried us away, tried to explain in the car — but she just looked confused. when her aunt excitedly showed us her new haircut, my daughter studied it and said, 'that looks weird. your old hair was better.' she told her teacher, in front of the whole class, that her breath smelled bad. she asked a classmate, during lunch, why his teeth were so crooked. she informed a pregnant family friend that she looked like she swallowed a basketball. she asked an elderly neighbor why he walks so slow and whether he's going to die soon. every single comment is delivered with complete innocence. she's not trying to be mean — i know that. there's no malice in her voice, no teasing expression. she genuinely seems to think she's just stating facts or asking legitimate questions. the sky is blue, grass is green, that person is overweight. all equally neutral observations to her. but she doesn't see the hurt on people's faces. she doesn't notice the uncomfortable silence that follows. she doesn't understand why everyone suddenly won't look at her. when i try to explain afterwards why these things are hurtful, she gets defensive and confused. 'but it's true!' she'll say, genuinely baffled. 'grandma is old. that man is fat. why can't i say true things?' she doesn't understand the difference between true and appropriate. she doesn't grasp that some truths are private, or unkind, or simply unnecessary to voice. i've tried explaining about feelings — how words can hurt even if they're accurate. i've tried explaining about privacy — how some observations about people's bodies are not our business. i've tried explaining about 'things we think but don't say' — how there's a filter between our thoughts and our words. nothing sticks. the next social situation, another comment. i'm constantly anxious now. every family gathering, every playdate, every public outing — i'm waiting for it. i try to preempt: 'remember, we don't comment on people's bodies.' but it doesn't help. the comments keep coming. other parents avoid us. i've seen them steer their children away. family gatherings have become tense — everyone on edge, waiting for what she'll say next. her friendships suffer because she says things that hurt other children's feelings, and they don't understand that she doesn't mean to be cruel. why doesn't my child understand that some thoughts should stay inside? why can't she see the impact of her words? and how do i teach this invisible social skill?

acceptedAnswer.text: SOCIAL FILTERING DIFFICULTIES / PRAGMATIC LANGUAGE DEFICITS / SOCIAL COGNITION CHALLENGES / PERSPECTIVE-TAKING DIFFICULTIES / VERBAL IMPULSE CONTROL DEFICITS! Making inappropriate comments — saying things that are hurtful, embarrassing, socially off-putting, or contextually wrong without apparent awareness of the impact — reflects challenges in social cognition, pragmatic language, and often impulse control. The child may struggle with perspective-taking (understanding how others will feel), social filtering (knowing which thoughts to voice and which to keep private), context reading (understanding what's appropriate in different situations), or verbal impulse control (stopping words before they come out). While young children commonly make inappropriate comments as they learn social rules, persistent inappropriate commenting beyond early childhood often indicates underlying difficulties that benefit from explicit teaching. These children typically don't intend harm — they genuinely don't understand the social rules they're breaking or can't apply them in the moment.

mainEntity.name: What is Social Filtering Difficulties / Pragmatic Language Deficits / Social Cognition Challenges / Perspective-Taking Difficulties / Verbal Impulse Control Deficits?

acceptedAnswer.text: Making inappropriate comments — saying things that are hurtful, embarrassing, socially off-putting, or contextually wrong without apparent awareness of the impact — reflects challenges in social cognition, pragmatic language, and often impulse control. The child may struggle with perspective-taking (understanding how others will feel), social filtering (knowing which thoughts to voice and which to keep private), context reading (understanding what's appropriate in different situations), or verbal impulse control (stopping words before they come out). While young children commonly make inappropriate comments as they learn social rules, persistent inappropriate commenting beyond early childhood often indicates underlying difficulties that benefit from explicit teaching. These children typically don't intend harm — they genuinely don't understand the social rules they're breaking or can't apply them in the moment. Common signs include: Comments on people's physical appearance (weight, age, skin, features), Points out differences in ways that embarrass others, Asks personal questions that violate privacy, States negative observations as neutral facts, Seems surprised when others are offended.

mainEntity.name: How does a Expected vs. Unexpected Behavior Charts help with social filtering difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Expected vs. Unexpected behavior charts (from the Social Thinking® methodology) help children understand that behaviors and comments can be 'expected' (what people anticipate and feel comfortable with) or 'unexpected' (what surprises people and makes them uncomfortable). This framework shifts away from 'good/bad' or 'right/wrong' language, which can feel shaming, toward a more neutral observation about social expectations. Children learn that unexpected comments make others have uncomfortable thoughts about us, which affects how they want to interact with us. The visual chart allows categorizing specific comments as expected or unexpected, making abstract social rules concrete. This framework helps children who think in terms of 'true/false' understand there's another dimension: 'expected/unexpected in this situation.' Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Create a two-column chart: 'Expected' and 'Unexpected.' Populate with specific examples from the child's life. 'When you see someone's new haircut, expected: say something nice or say nothing. Unexpected: point out what you don't like.' Use real situations the child has encountered. Safety: Frame as learning about social expectations, not as judgment of the child's character. The goal is understanding, not shame.

mainEntity.name: How does a Thought Bubble vs. Speech Bubble Visuals help with social filtering difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Thought bubble vs. speech bubble visuals teach the fundamental concept that we have two kinds of thoughts: ones we keep in our heads (thought bubbles) and ones we say out loud (speech bubbles). This visual metaphor helps children understand that not every thought needs to become a spoken word. Many children who make inappropriate comments haven't learned this distinction — to them, thoughts and speech are the same thing. Seeing the visual separation (thought bubble stays inside head, speech bubble comes out of mouth) makes the abstract concept concrete. Practice activities involve sorting comments: 'Is this a thought bubble or a speech bubble comment?' Over time, children learn to pause and ask themselves: 'Should this stay in my thought bubble or come out in my speech bubble?' Price: ₹100–500. DIY option: Draw or print thought bubbles and speech bubbles. Create cards with different comments. Practice sorting: 'Grandma has wrinkles' — thought bubble or speech bubble? 'I like your dress' — thought bubble or speech bubble? Make it interactive and low-stakes. Safety: Emphasize that having thoughts is okay — even unkind thoughts are normal. The skill is in choosing what to say, not in never having thoughts.

mainEntity.name: How does a Social Stories About Appropriate Comments help with social filtering difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social Stories® (developed by Carol Gray) provide clear, explicit explanations of social situations from the child's perspective. For inappropriate comments, social stories explain what kinds of comments help people feel good, what kinds of comments hurt even if they're true, why some observations should stay private, and how to decide what to say. The stories address specific situations the child encounters — commenting on bodies, asking personal questions, sharing private family information — and provide concrete guidance. Social stories work because they don't rely on implicit social learning; they make the unwritten rules explicit. For children who genuinely don't understand why 'but it's true' isn't sufficient justification, social stories explain the 'why' in concrete terms. Price: ₹200–1,000. DIY option: Write personalized social stories using Carol Gray's formula. Example: 'Sometimes I notice things about people's bodies. (Descriptive) Other people usually don't like when we talk about their bodies. (Perspective) It can hurt their feelings, even if what I notice is true. (Perspective) I can keep my observations in my thought bubble. (Directive) This helps people feel comfortable around me. (Affirmative)' Safety: Social stories should be read when calm, not as consequences. They are teaching tools, not punishment. Personalize to the child's specific situations.

mainEntity.name: How does a Perspective-Taking Games and Activities help with social filtering difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Perspective-taking games help children practice seeing situations from others' points of view — understanding how someone might feel when hearing different comments. This is the foundation of social filtering: if you can predict that a comment will hurt, you're more likely to keep it inside. Activities might include looking at photos of facial expressions and discussing what the person might be feeling, reading scenarios and predicting emotional reactions, role-playing receiving different kinds of comments, or playing games that require understanding others' knowledge or feelings. For children who don't naturally intuit others' perspectives, these structured practice opportunities build the skill explicitly. The goal is developing the mental habit of asking 'How would this make them feel?' before speaking. Price: ₹300–1,500. DIY option: Use family photos or magazine pictures showing emotions. Ask 'How do you think they feel? What might have happened?' Read picture books and pause to discuss characters' feelings. Play 'How would you feel if someone said...' with various comment examples. Safety: Keep activities supportive, not accusatory. The goal is building a skill, not inducing guilt. Connect to the child's own experiences of having feelings hurt.

mainEntity.name: How does a Role-Play Scenarios and Scripts help with social filtering difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Role-play provides safe practice for navigating social situations where inappropriate comments might emerge. Children can rehearse what to say (and not say) when meeting someone new, when noticing something about someone's appearance, when asked for an opinion, or when curious about personal information. Scripts provide exact words for common situations: 'When Grandma shows me something she made, I can say: That's nice, Grandma. Even if I don't love it.' Role-play also allows children to experience the receiving end — what it feels like when someone makes an inappropriate comment about you. This experiential understanding often has more impact than explanation. Practicing appropriate responses in low-stakes play makes them more available in real situations. Price: ₹200–1,000. DIY option: Create scenario cards for common situations. Role-play with stuffed animals or puppets for younger children. Practice both 'what to say' and 'what to keep in your thought bubble.' Let the child sometimes play the person receiving comments to build empathy. Safety: Keep role-play light and supportive. If it becomes stressful or feels like criticism, it loses effectiveness. Celebrate successful practice.

mainEntity.name: How does a Size of the Problem Scales help with social filtering difficulties?

acceptedAnswer.text: Size of the problem scales help children calibrate their reactions and comments to the actual significance of situations. Many children who make inappropriate comments have difficulty distinguishing between small observations (someone has gray hair) and situations that warrant comment (someone is in danger). Everything feels equally worth remarking on. Visual scales showing small/medium/big/huge problems — with examples — help children understand that most observations about people's appearance are 'no problem' or 'tiny problem' that don't need to be mentioned. The scale provides a framework for asking: 'Is this something I need to say anything about?' Most inappropriate comments are about things that are tiny or not problems at all — not the child's business, not requiring comment. Price: ₹100–500. DIY option: Create a visual scale from 1-5 or 'tiny to huge.' Categorize examples: 'Someone has a wrinkled shirt — tiny, not my business. Someone is choking — huge, I should get help.' Practice with scenarios the child encounters. Safety: This isn't about dismissing the child's observations — it's about helping them evaluate what warrants spoken comment versus internal notation.

@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Social Filtering Difficulties
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Children who make inappropriate comments typically don't intend harm — they have genuine difficulty with social cognition, perspective-taking, verbal filtering, or impulse control. The solution involves explicit teaching of social rules that neurotypical children absorb implicitly, visual supports that make abstract concepts concrete, perspective-taking practice that builds empathy, and structured rehearsal of appropriate alternatives. The filter between thinking and saying can be built through patient, systematic teaching.

citation.name: Research on Pragmatic Language Development and Social Cognition
citation.description: Research on pragmatic language development indicates that appropriate social commenting requires the integration of perspective-taking, social context reading, verbal filtering, and impulse control. Children with autism spectrum disorder, ADHD, social communication disorder, or pragmatic language differences often struggle with social filtering despite adequate vocabulary and grammar. Evidence-based intervention includes explicit teaching of social rules, visual supports, perspective-taking practice, and structured opportunities to rehearse appropriate responses.

item.name: Expected vs. Unexpected Behavior Charts
item.description: Expected vs. Unexpected behavior charts (from the Social Thinking® methodology) help children understand that behaviors and comments can be 'expected' (what people anticipate and feel comfortable with) or 'unexpected' (what surprises people and makes them uncomfortable). This framework shifts away from 'good/bad' or 'right/wrong' language, which can feel shaming, toward a more neutral observation about social expectations. Children learn that unexpected comments make others have uncomfortable thoughts about us, which affects how they want to interact with us. The visual chart allows categorizing specific comments as expected or unexpected, making abstract social rules concrete. This framework helps children who think in terms of 'true/false' understand there's another dimension: 'expected/unexpected in this situation.'

'When you see someone's new haircut, expected: say something nice or say nothing. Unexpected: point out what you don't like.' Use real situations the child has encountered.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note

Over time, children learn to pause and ask themselves: 'Should this stay in my thought bubble or come out in my speech bubble?'

Scripts provide exact words for common situations: 'When Grandma shows me something she made, I can say: That's nice, Grandma. Even if I don't love it.' Role-play also allows children to experience the receiving end — what it feels like when someone makes an inappropriate comment about you. This experiential understanding often has more impact than explanation. Practicing appropriate responses in low-stakes play makes them more available in real situations.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create scenario cards for common situations. Role-play with stuffed animals or puppets for younger children. Practice both 'what to say' and 'what to keep in your thought bubble.' Let the child sometimes play the person receiving comments to build empathy.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Keep role-play light and supportive. If it becomes stressful or feels like criticism, it loses effectiveness. Celebrate successful practice.

item.name: Size of the Problem Scales
item.description: Size of the problem scales help children calibrate their reactions and comments to the actual significance of situations. Many children who make inappropriate comments have difficulty distinguishing between small observations (someone has gray hair) and situations that warrant comment (someone is in danger). Everything feels equally worth remarking on. Visual scales showing small/medium/big/huge problems — with examples — help children understand that most observations about people's appearance are 'no problem' or 'tiny problem' that don't need to be mentioned. The scale provides a framework for asking: 'Is this something I need to say anything about?' Most inappropriate comments are about things that are tiny or not problems at all — not the child's business, not requiring comment.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a visual scale from 1-5 or 'tiny to huge.' Categorize examples: 'Someone has a wrinkled shirt — tiny, not my business. Someone is choking — huge, I should get help.' Practice with scenarios the child encounters.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: This isn't about dismissing the child's observations — it's about helping them evaluate what warrants spoken comment versus internal notation.

item.name: Compliment and Kind Words Card Sets
item.description: Compliment and kind words card sets provide children with a ready vocabulary of appropriate things to say in social situations. Rather than just avoiding inappropriate comments, children learn what TO say — positive observations, kind words, appropriate responses to others' news or appearance. This fills the gap that some children experience: they notice something about a person and feel compelled to comment, but don't know what appropriate comments sound like. Having practiced compliments and kind phrases available ('I like your shirt,' 'That's cool,' 'You're good at that') gives the child something to say that meets their need to comment while being socially appropriate. The cards can be practiced, memorized, and deployed as go-to responses.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create cards with compliments for different situations: 'When someone shows me something they made: Wow, that's cool! or You worked hard on that!' When meeting someone: 'Nice to meet you.' Practice until phrases become natural.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Compliments should feel genuine, not forced. Help children find kind words they can mean. Scripted doesn't have to mean fake.

item.name: Comic Strip Conversations
item.description: Comic strip conversations (developed by Carol Gray) use simple drawings with speech bubbles and thought bubbles to work through social situations. The visual format shows what people said, what they might have been thinking, and how they felt — making the internal world visible. For inappropriate comments, comic strip conversations can illustrate: what the child said, what the other person thought/felt (shown in thought bubbles), what the social outcome was, and what the child could say instead. This visual processing often works better than verbal explanation alone for children who struggle with social cognition. Creating comic strips together about real incidents helps children understand the impact of their words in a concrete, non-threatening format.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use blank comic strip templates or simple drawings. Draw stick figures with speech bubbles (what they said) and thought bubbles (what they felt/thought). Different colors can represent different emotions. Work through real situations the child encountered.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Use comic strips to understand and plan, not to shame. Focus forward on 'what could work better' rather than dwelling on mistakes.

item.name: Private vs. Public Information Sorting Activities
item.description: Private vs. public information sorting activities teach children to distinguish between information that's okay to share or discuss publicly versus information that should stay private. Many inappropriate comments involve sharing private information — about their own family ('My mom and dad had a big fight'), about others' bodies or personal matters ('Why does that lady only have one arm?'), or asking questions about private topics ('How much money does your family have?'). Sorting activities help children categorize: What's public (okay to talk about with anyone)? What's private to family (only share at home)? What's private to the person (none of my business to mention)? This framework helps children understand that not all observations need to be voiced, and some questions shouldn't be asked.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create sorting mats labeled 'Okay to talk about anywhere,' 'Only share at home,' 'Private — not my business.' Make cards with different topics and practice sorting: 'What I ate for breakfast' (public), 'That person's body size' (private — not my business), 'Family arguments' (only at home).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Privacy teaching should protect the child too — help them understand what information about themselves is private and shouldn't be demanded by others.

socialMedia.caption: True. But should you SAY it? When your child says things that make rooms go silent — comments about bodies, appearances, differences, delivered with complete innocence — the filter between thinking and saying needs building. Here are 9 materials that help: expected vs. unexpected charts, thought bubble vs. speech bubble visuals, social stories, perspective-taking games, role-play scripts, size of the problem scales, kind words cards, comic strip conversations, and private vs. public sorting. Building the filter that turns thoughts into appropriate words. Save for teaching social awareness! 🔖 #InappropriateComments #SocialFilter #PinnacleBlooms
